content.text: Python's four built-in collection types — lists, tuples, sets, and dicts — cover virtually every data organization need in Python programs. Choosing the right collection type has direct implications for correctness, performance, and readability.
A collection is a Python object that holds multiple other objects. Python's built-in collections are:
(ordered key-value mapping, O(1) lookup). Python 3.7+ guarantees dict preserves insertion order.
syntax and comprehensions. Lists, tuples, and dicts support slicing and indexing; sets and dict keys support O(1)
checks. Understanding when to use each one — and when to reach for the
module — separates readable, performant Python from naive code.

Choosing the wrong collection type is one of the most common sources of performance problems and subtle bugs in Python. Using a list where a set is appropriate makes membership testing O(n) instead of O(1). Using a list where a dict is appropriate requires iterating to find items instead of O(1) lookup. Using a mutable list where a tuple should be used allows accidental mutation of data that should be fixed.

For agentic AI systems, the right collection at each layer matters: a set for visited URLs prevents an agent from recrawling pages; a dict for tool call arguments enables named access; a deque instead of a list for a message buffer provides O(1) append-right and pop-left. These are not micro-optimizations — at scale they are the difference between a pipeline that runs in seconds and one that times out.

Collections are also the primary format for JSON exchange with LLM APIs: JSON objects become dicts, JSON arrays become lists, JSON null becomes None. Understanding how to construct, access, transform, and validate these structures is essential for any code that consumes API responses or builds API payloads.
Lists support O(1) indexed access, O(1) amortized append, O(1) pop from the right, and full slice notation — the most flexible general-purpose sequence type.
Tuples are hashable when all elements are hashable, making them usable as dict keys and set members, enabling compound-key lookups and record deduplication.
Sets provide O(1) average membership testing, automatic deduplication, and full set algebra (union
Dicts provide O(1) average key lookup, insertion, and deletion.
avoids KeyError for missing keys. Dict comprehensions and merge operators make construction concise.
All four support iteration and comprehensions with the same
module provides production-ready specialized types that outperform hand-rolled solutions.
membership testing and O(n) for insertion/deletion at arbitrary positions. Do not use a list as a set for repeated membership checks on large data.
Sets and dict keys must be hashable — mutable objects (lists, dicts, sets) cannot be members. Compound keys must be tuples; mutable data cannot be deduplicated without conversion.
Dicts maintain insertion order but are not sorted. For sorted access, use
Tuples are immutable: you cannot append, insert, or remove elements. When the sequence needs to change, use a list.
Sets are unordered — you cannot index into a set or rely on iteration order.
Python collections have significant per-object overhead. For large collections of simple numbers, NumPy arrays use much less memory and are far faster for bulk operations.

Lists are dynamic arrays. The underlying C array is over-allocated with a growth factor (~1.125x per resize) to amortize resizing cost. This makes append O(1) amortized but means allocated capacity may exceed current length. Indexed access is O(1) via pointer arithmetic. Insertion or deletion at an arbitrary index is O(n) because all elements after the point must be shifted.
Python dicts and sets are hash tables with open addressing. Inserting a key computes

to find the initial slot; on collision, neighboring slots are probed until an empty slot is found. The load factor is kept below ~2/3 by resizing. This gives O(1) average for insert, lookup, and delete — worst case O(n) with adversarial hash collisions. Python 3.3+ uses per-process hash randomization for strings to prevent collision attacks.

Python 3.6 introduced a compact dict that separates the index array from the key-value array, preserving insertion order while reducing memory. This is the basis of the Python 3.7 order-preservation guarantee.

Tuples are fixed-size C arrays allocated once. Because they are immutable, they require no over-allocation and use less memory than lists. CPython caches empty tuples and single-element tuples as singletons. Tuples are hashable when all elements are, enabling their use as dict keys and set members.
The diagram shows all four collection types with their properties, complexity characteristics, and typical use cases.

attrs.alt: Python collections: list, tuple, set, and dict with key properties and collections module extras

content.text: : Use when order matters, duplicates are possible, and the collection will be modified. The default choice for ordered sequences.
: Use for fixed-length records where position has semantic meaning — (x, y) coordinate, (name, age) record. Use as dict keys for compound key lookups.
: Use when membership testing is the primary operation, order does not matter, and duplicates must not exist. Convert a list to set to deduplicate.
: Use when associating keys with values and looking up by key. Use for JSON data, API payloads, configuration, and any map-like data structure.
: Use instead of list when O(1) append and pop from both ends are needed — queues, sliding windows, BFS.
: Use for frequency counting — faster and more convenient than a manual defaultdict(int) loop.
: Use when building a dict by incrementally adding to the value for each key, avoiding the check-then-add pattern.

# --- list ---
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
nums.append(5)                  # O(1) amortized
nums.insert(0, 0)               # O(n) — shifts all elements right
nums.remove(1)                  # removes first 1; O(n)
nums.pop()                      # O(1) — last element
nums.pop(0)                     # O(n) — first element (use deque for O(1))
print(sorted(nums))             # new sorted list; original unchanged
nums.sort(reverse=True)         # sort in place
print(nums[2:5])                # slice: indices 2, 3, 4
print(nums[::-1])               # reverse

# --- tuple ---
point = (3.0, 4.0)
x, y = point                   # unpacking
first, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) # star unpacking
print(first, rest)              # 1 [2, 3, 4, 5]
lookup = {(0, 0): "origin", (1, 0): "right"}
print(lookup[(0, 0)])           # "origin"

# --- set ---
unique = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1}
print(unique)                   # {1, 2, 3}
unique.add(4)
unique.discard(10)              # no error if not present
print(3 in unique)              # True — O(1)
a = {1, 2, 3, 4}
b = {3, 4, 5, 6}
print(a | b)                    # {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} — union
print(a & b)                    # {3, 4}             — intersection
print(a - b)                    # {1, 2}             — difference
print(a ^ b)                    # {1, 2, 5, 6}       — symmetric diff

# --- dict ---
d = {"name": "Alice", "score": 92}
print(d["name"])                # "Alice"
print(d.get("age", 0))         # 0 — safe default
d["age"] = 30
del d["score"]
d.pop("age", None)             # safe remove

for key, val in d.items():
    print(f"{key}={val}")

# Merge (Python 3.9+)
defaults = {"timeout": 30, "retries": 3}
overrides = {"timeout": 60}
merged = defaults | overrides   # overrides win
print(merged)                   # {"timeout": 60, "retries": 3}

from collections import Counter, defaultdict, deque, namedtuple

# Counter
tokens = ["the", "cat", "sat", "on", "the", "mat", "the"]
freq = Counter(tokens)
print(freq.most_common(2))     # [('the', 3), ('cat', 1)]
print(freq["dog"])             # 0 — missing keys return 0

c1 = Counter(a=3, b=1)
c2 = Counter(a=1, b=2, c=5)
print(c1 + c2)   # Counter({'c': 5, 'a': 4, 'b': 3})

# defaultdict
word_positions = defaultdict(list)
for pos, token in enumerate(tokens):
    word_positions[token].append(pos)
print(dict(word_positions))
# {'the': [0, 4, 6], 'cat': [1], ...}

# Nested defaultdict
nested = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(int))
for group, item in [("A", "x"), ("A", "y"), ("B", "x"), ("A", "x")]:
    nested[group][item] += 1
print(dict(nested))  # {'A': {'x': 2, 'y': 1}, 'B': {'x': 1}}

# deque with maxlen
queue = deque(maxlen=3)
for x in ["a", "b", "c", "d"]:
    queue.append(x)               # "a" auto-dropped when "d" is added
print(queue)                      # deque(['b', 'c', 'd'], maxlen=3)

buf = deque([1, 2, 3])
buf.appendleft(0)                 # O(1) — not O(n) like list.insert(0,...)
print(buf.popleft())              # 0 — O(1)

# namedtuple
Point = namedtuple("Point", ["x", "y"])
p = Point(3.0, 4.0)
print(p.x, p.y)                   # 3.0 4.0
print(p._asdict())                 # {'x': 3.0, 'y': 4.0}
print(p._replace(x=10.0))         # Point(x=10.0, y=4.0)

from collections import deque

def crawl(seed_urls: list[str], max_pages: int = 100) -> list[str]:
    """BFS with O(1) deduplication using sets."""
    visited = set()
    queue = deque(seed_urls)
    discovered = []

    while queue and len(visited) < max_pages:
        url = queue.popleft()         # O(1) deque pop
        if url in visited:            # O(1) set membership
            continue
        visited.add(url)              # O(1) set insert
        discovered.append(url)
        # In real code, fetch and parse links here
        for link in [url + "/a", url + "/b", seed_urls[0]]:
            if link not in visited:
                queue.append(link)
    return discovered

result = crawl(["https://example.com"], max_pages=4)
print(result)

# Set algebra for batch URL filtering
new_urls  = {"http://a.com", "http://b.com", "http://c.com"}
known_urls = {"http://a.com", "http://d.com"}
to_fetch = new_urls - known_urls   # difference: new not yet seen
print(to_fetch)   # {'http://b.com', 'http://c.com'}

A token vocabulary analyzer for LLM evaluation combines all four collection types: a list for the token stream, a Counter for frequency, a set for vocabulary deduplication, and a dict for per-token statistics.

from collections import Counter
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class VocabStats:
    total_tokens: int
    unique_tokens: int
    top_10: list[tuple[str, int]]
    coverage: dict[str, float]

def analyze_tokens(token_stream: list[str]) -> VocabStats:
    freq = Counter(token_stream)           # dict subclass, O(n) to build
    vocab = set(freq.keys())               # unique tokens
    total = len(token_stream)
    top_10 = freq.most_common(10)
    coverage = {token: count / total
                for token, count in freq.items()}
    return VocabStats(len(token_stream), len(vocab), top_10, coverage)

tokens = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the fox".split()
stats = analyze_tokens(tokens)
print(f"Total: {stats.total_tokens}, Unique: {stats.unique_tokens}")
print("Top 3:", stats.top_10[:3])
print(f"'the' coverage: {stats.coverage.get('the', 0):.1%}")

# Set vocabulary comparison
v1 = {"python", "async", "await", "type"}
v2 = {"python", "walrus", "match", "type"}
print("Shared:", v1 & v2)
print("Only v1:", v1 - v2)
print("Only v2:", v2 - v1)
Mistake 1: List for Membership Testing in a Loop

items = list(range(10000))

# WRONG: O(n^2) total
seen = []
unique = []
for item in items:
    if item not in seen:   # O(n) each iteration
        seen.append(item)
        unique.append(item)

# CORRECT: O(n) total
seen = set()
unique = []
for item in items:
    if item not in seen:   # O(1) each iteration
        seen.add(item)
        unique.append(item)

# SIMPLEST when order not needed:
unique = list(set(items))

# PRESERVING insertion order (Python 3.7+):
unique = list(dict.fromkeys(items))  # O(n)
Mistake 2: Mutating a Dict While Iterating

# WRONG: shared list between calls
def add_to_history(item, history=[]):
    history.append(item)
    return history

print(add_to_history("a"))  # ['a']
print(add_to_history("b"))  # ['a', 'b'] — unexpected!

# CORRECT
def add_to_history_fixed(item, history=None):
    if history is None:
        history = []
    history.append(item)
    return history
for O(1) right-end adds. For O(1) both-end adds/removes, use
Convert a list to a set before repeated membership checks. O(n) to build; O(1) per lookup. Pays for itself after the second check.
for top-N — it uses a heap and is O(n + k log n), faster than O(n log n) full sort.
Never modify a dict or list while iterating it. Use a list comprehension or iterate over a copy.
is an immutable hashable set — use it when a set must be used as a dict key or set member.
for explicit, readable iteration rather than manual index lookups.

For numerical data, prefer NumPy arrays over Python lists. A NumPy array of 1 million float64 values uses 8 MB (8 bytes each, contiguous). A Python list of 1 million floats uses ~30 MB (56 bytes per float object + 8 bytes per pointer, fragmented). NumPy arithmetic is also ~100x faster for bulk operations.
: O(n) frequency table from any iterable. Supports arithmetic (add, subtract, intersection of counts) and
: Like dict but with a factory for missing keys. Avoids the 'check if key exists then initialize' pattern.
: Double-ended queue with O(1) append/pop on both ends. Supports maxlen for bounded circular buffers.
: Lightweight record type; still a tuple (indexable, hashable, unpackable). Add methods via subclassing.
: Class with auto-generated __init__, __repr__, __eq__. Better than namedtuple when mutable fields or methods are needed.

# sortedcontainers: sorted list/dict/set with O(log n) insert
# pip install sortedcontainers
from sortedcontainers import SortedList
sl = SortedList([3, 1, 4, 1, 5])
sl.add(2)
print(list(sl))   # [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

# attrs / pydantic: validated data containers
# pip install pydantic
from pydantic import BaseModel
class ToolParams(BaseModel):
    query: str
    limit: int = 10
p = ToolParams(query="python", limit=5)
print(p.model_dump())  # {'query': 'python', 'limit': 5}
When membership is the primary operation

# WRONG: aliasing
config_a = {"timeout": 30}
config_b = config_a
config_b["timeout"] = 60
print(config_a["timeout"])  # 60 — unintended mutation!

# CORRECT: copy
config_b = config_a.copy()           # shallow copy
# or: config_b = {**config_a}
# or: config_b = dict(config_a)
config_b["timeout"] = 60
print(config_a["timeout"])   # 30 — unaffected

# For nested structures, deep copy
import copy
config_c = copy.deepcopy(config_a)

Python 3.3+ randomizes string hashes per-process (PYTHONHASHSEED). This prevents adversarially crafted keys from all hashing to the same bucket, which would turn O(1) dict operations into O(n) and cause denial of service. Never disable hash randomization in production services that accept user-controlled dict keys.
for optional keys in dicts, and validate list indices when they come from user input. Unhandled
in an agentic loop will crash the agent mid-task without the ability to retry.

Think of a list as a numbered parking lot: slot 0, slot 1, slot 2 — you can park anything in any slot, add a new slot at the end cheaply, but removing a slot from the middle requires everything else to shuffle.
A tuple is a sealed box with labeled compartments. Sealed at creation, it can receive a barcode (hash) and be used to label other boxes (dict key, set member).
A set is a bag where every item automatically receives a unique address (hash). You cannot reach in and grab 'the third item' — you only ask 'is X in here?' and get an instant yes/no.
A dict is a lookup table with a hash index: hand the librarian a key, the librarian looks it up in O(1) steps regardless of whether the library has 10 or 10 million entries.
for unordered unique collections with O(1) membership;
operator is O(n) for lists/tuples, O(1) avg for sets/dict keys. Converting a list to a set before repeated membership tests is the single most impactful performance change in many Python programs.
Never modify a collection while iterating it. Use a list comprehension or iterate over a copy.
) of a mutable collection creates an alias, not a copy. Use
— production-ready specialized types that outperform hand-rolled equivalents.
Tuples are hashable (if all elements are) and serve as compound dict keys. Use
Dicts are insertion-ordered in Python 3.7+. For sorted access, use
rather than maintaining sorted order in the dict itself.
For numerical bulk data, NumPy arrays use ~4x less memory and are ~100x faster than Python lists of numbers for arithmetic operations.
